title: How the body stores the shock of sudden loss differently than chronic abuse
author: Kalesh

Two different kinds of pain 

 I've been thinking a lot about how we talk about trauma. We use this one word for so many different experiences. And I thin...
htmlContent:
Two different kinds of pain

I've been thinking a lot about how we talk about trauma. We use this one word for so many different experiences. And I think that's a problem. Because the way your body responds to a single catastrophic event is not the same as how it responds to years of slow, grinding abuse. They feel different in the body. They create different patterns. And they need different kinds of healing.

Here's the thing. I've worked with people who lost a partner suddenly in a car accident. And I've worked with people who grew up with a parent who was emotionally unpredictable, sometimes loving, sometimes cruel. Both carry trauma. But if you put them in a room together, they would describe their physical experiences completely differently.

Let's talk about why that is.

Peter Levine, the founder of Somatic Experiencing, spent decades watching how animals in the wild shake off life-threatening experiences. They don't get traumatized. They discharge the energy. But humans? We get stuck. And the way we get stuck depends on the nature of the threat.

The body's response to sudden loss

Sudden loss hits like a lightning strike. One moment your world is intact. The next moment it's not. Your nervous system doesn't have time to prepare. There's no warning. No gradual adjustment. Just a massive spike of activation that has nowhere to go.

I remember a client who lost her brother to a heart attack. He was 34. They had spoken on the phone the night before. She described the phone call from his wife, the way the words didn't make sense at first, the way her body went cold. For months afterward, she would wake up in the middle of the night with her heart pounding. She couldn't catch her breath. Her muscles felt locked, like she was bracing for another blow.

That's the signature of shock trauma. It's stored in the body as a kind of frozen alertness. Your nervous system is still waiting for the threat to pass. But the threat already happened. The problem is your body doesn't know that.

Bessel van der Kolk, who wrote The Body Keeps the Score (paid link), has shown that traumatic memories aren't stored in the narrative parts of the brain. They're stored in the body. In the amygdala. In the autonomic nervous system. In the muscles and the fascia. For sudden loss, the body holds a specific pattern: high sympathetic activation with no completion. The fight or flight response got triggered but never finished.

Think about it. If a tiger chases you and you escape, your body discharges the energy. You shake. You pant. You cry. But if the tiger disappears in a flash and you never get to run or fight or even scream, that energy stays locked in your tissues. That's sudden loss. The tiger is gone. But your body is still running from it.

I've seen this pattern over and over. People with sudden loss trauma often have a specific quality to their tension. It's not diffuse. It's concentrated. Tight shoulders. A clenched jaw. A feeling of being "on" all the time. They startle easily. They have trouble relaxing. Their bodies are still in that moment of impact, waiting for permission to let go.

The body's response to chronic abuse

Chronic abuse is a different animal entirely. It's not a lightning strike. It's acid rain. Slow. Corrosive. Constant. Your nervous system doesn't get a single massive hit. It gets thousands of small hits, day after day, year after year. And each one teaches your body something terrible: there is no safe place. There is no escape. This is just how life is.

When I work with survivors of chronic childhood abuse, I see a different physical pattern. It's not the frozen alertness of shock trauma. It's a collapse. A giving up. The body learns to shut down because staying activated all the time would kill you. So you dissociate. You go numb. You learn to leave your body because being in your body is unbearable.

Janina Fisher, who writes about trauma and the body, talks about how chronic abuse creates a fragmented self. Parts of you go into hiding. Parts of you learn to comply. Parts of you become hypervigilant to other people's moods. Your nervous system develops a kind of chaotic rhythm. Sometimes you're numb. Sometimes you're flooded. You never know which version of yourself is going to show up.

Stay with me here. Because this is where it gets specific.

Chronic abuse survivors often have a collapsed posture. Rounded shoulders. A forward head. A shallow, high-chest breathing pattern. Their bodies look like they're trying to take up as little space as possible. Because that's what kept them safe. If you're small and quiet and invisible, maybe the abuse will stop. Maybe they won't notice you.

There's also a different quality to the muscle tension. In shock trauma, the tension is hard and rigid. Like armor. In chronic abuse, the tension is more like a low-grade holding pattern. The muscles aren't braced for impact. They're braced for endurance. For waiting. For surviving another day.

I remember a client who had been sexually abused by an uncle from age 6 to 14. She described her body as "a heavy coat she couldn't take off." She felt disconnected from her legs, her pelvis, her belly. Those parts of her body were too dangerous to inhabit. They held too much memory. So she lived in her head. She was smart and articulate and could talk about anything. But ask her what she felt in her body, and she would go blank. Nothing. Just nothing.

That's the signature of chronic abuse trauma. It's not about being stuck in high activation. It's about being stuck in disconnection. Your body is there. But you're not really in it.

Why the distinction matters for healing

I see so many people get frustrated with their healing because they're using the wrong approach. They read a book about trauma that talks about shaking and discharging and completing the fight response. And they try it. And it doesn't work. Or it makes things worse. And they think something is wrong with them.

But here's the thing. If your trauma is chronic abuse, shaking and discharging might not be what you need first. You might need to learn how to feel your body at all. You might need to build the capacity to tolerate sensation without immediately dissociating. You might need to work very slowly, very gently, with the parts of you that learned to disappear.

For sudden loss survivors, the work is often about completing the response that got interrupted. Helping the body move through the freeze. Allowing the shaking and the tears and the sounds that didn't get to come out at the time. A Breathing Exercise Tool (paid link) that helps slow the exhale can be genuinely useful here, because it signals to the nervous system that it's safe to come out of high alert. The extended exhale activates the vagus nerve and tells the body, "We made it. We're okay now."

For chronic abuse survivors, the work is often about re-inhabiting the body piece by piece. Learning to feel your feet on the floor. Learning to notice your breath without trying to change it. Learning that sensation doesn't have to mean danger. A tool that many find helpful for releasing stored body tension is the TriggerPoint GRID Foam Roller (paid link), but you have to use it differently. Not aggressively. Not trying to "break up" the tension. Just slowly, gently, bringing awareness to the places that have been held tight for so long.

For those exploring somatic approaches, a book worth considering is Waking the Tiger (paid link) by Peter Levine. It explains the mechanism of how animals discharge trauma and how humans can do the same. But I would say this: if you're a chronic abuse survivor, read it with caution. The techniques are powerful. And powerful things need to be approached slowly when your system has learned to survive by being disconnected.

What both types of trauma share

I don't want to create a false separation. Both sudden loss and chronic abuse are traumatic. Both live in the body. Both require attention to the nervous system. Both can heal.

But the pathway is different. And pretending it's the same does a disservice to survivors.

What both types share is this: the body is telling a story that words cannot fully express. Your tight shoulders, your shallow breath, your numb pelvis, your clenched jaw, your racing heart, your empty stomach. These are not random. They are the language of your history. They are the places where your experience got lodged.

Stephen Porges, who developed Polyvagal Theory, talks about how the nervous system is constantly scanning for safety and danger. When you've experienced trauma, your scanner gets stuck. It keeps seeing danger even when the danger is gone. Or it shuts down completely and stops scanning at all. Both are survival strategies. Both were necessary at one time. And both can change.

I think the most important thing I can tell you is this: your body is not broken. It's not wrong. It's not defective. It adapted to what you went through. It found a way to get you through. And now, with the right kind of help, with the right kind of attention, it can learn to adapt again.

But it needs you to understand what happened. Not just in your head. In your bones. In your tissues. In the places where the memory lives without words.

Sit with that.

Notice what just happened in your body as you read that. Did your breath change? Did your shoulders tighten? Did you feel a pull toward your phone, toward distraction? That's your system doing what it learned to do. That's not a problem. That's information.

The healing doesn't come from forcing your body to be different. It comes from learning to listen. From learning to be with what's there. From learning that you can feel your body and survive. You can feel the tightness and the numbness and the racing and the emptiness. And none of it will destroy you.

Because the thing that happened already happened. You survived it. Now the question is not whether you can survive. You already did. The question is whether you can learn to live. To really live. In this body that carried you through hell.

I believe you can. I believe your body can learn new things. I believe the patterns that got locked in can . Not because I've read the research, though I have. But because I've seen it happen. I've seen the frozen start to thaw. I've seen the numb start to feel. I've seen the collapsed posture slowly, slowly begin to open.

It takes time. It takes patience. It takes the right approach for what actually happened to you.

But it's possible.

And that's not a platitude. That's a truth I've watched unfold in real bodies, in real lives, including my own.

Healing Journey Resources

  • Breathing Exercise Tool (paid link) - A tool for slowing the exhale and supporting nervous system regulation
  • The Body Keeps the Score (paid link) - Bessel van der Kolk's foundational book on how trauma lives in the body
  • TriggerPoint GRID Foam Roller (paid link) - A tool for gentle, mindful release of stored body tension
  • Waking the Tiger (paid link) - Peter Levine's exploration of how animals and humans heal from trauma
